title: AI Agent Governance Lacking in 87% of Organizations - LinkedIn
search_path_label: A 级媒体 / GDELT 路径
full_text:
AI Agent Governance Lacking in 87% of Organizations
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Jacquie Hudson
3w
Report this post
Only 13% of organizations feel they have adequate AI agent governance in place, according to Gartner via today’s WSJ piece on AI agent sprawl.
FICO’s Mike Trkay , CIO and Chief Customer Officer, shared with Belle L. how new agents are being created daily at every level of our organization. That’s a sign of how embedded AI has become in our work, alongside a focus on governance to ensure reliable, consistent results at scale.
Timely, since agentic AI is one of the conversations happening at #FICOWorld next week.
Worth a read: https://lnkd.in/eTCbm-7H
34
5 Comments
Like
Comment
Share
Copy
LinkedIn
Facebook
Alex Cinovoj
2w
Report this comment
13% feels believable.
Agent adoption is moving faster than the operating model around it. The winners will not be the teams with the most agents. It will be the teams with the clearest inventory, ownership, permissions, logs, and shutdown paths.
Like
Reply
1 Reaction
2 Reactions
Kayla Leong
2w
Report this comment
The development of AI agents is proceeding at such a rapid pace that the critical focus has now shifted to governance capabilities. FICO notes that new agents are emerging daily a clear indication that AI is truly becoming integrated into workflows
Like
Reply
1 Reaction
2 Reactions
Linda Gilleran
2w
Report this comment
#IBMWatsonxOrchestrate
Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Abel Baruwa
2w
Report this comment
AI agent sprawl becoming a problem just proves governance was never built into deployment, and now teams are paying shadow IT costs in compute instead of SaaS licenses.
https://www.projectaictch.com/pipeline
Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Brian Hodak
2w
Report this comment
The core issue facing enterprises is not "AI agent sprawl," but rather unmanaged digital actors that possess autonomy, context, and identity, creating risk when increasing autonomy meets decreasing visibility. To manage this, agents must be treated as enterprise actors with governance built on four pillars: orchestration, lineage, execution controls, and validation.
Like

Paul Dixon
3w
Report this post
From the article,"In the next two years, the average global Fortune 500 enterprise will run over 150,000 AI agents, according to market research and IT consulting firm Gartner. But only 13% of organizations think they have adequate AI agent governance in place, Gartner said. " Bad enough that insight into AI tools is spotty, now we have to worry about too many AI agents? What is a CIO/CISO to do? AI Drive by Nexthink.

Most firms are encouraging AI experimentation internally right now, but eventually they’ll have to deal with the operational mess that comes with thousands of agents scattered across the organization.

Interesting read on a new area of AI governance: the AI sprawl. Gartner estimates that in 2 years, the average fortune 500 company will have over 150,000 agents and agent governance has yet to catch up with this growth. As companies invest in AI training across their firms, the need for greater governance will increase.

Flavio A. Carosio Zanetti
3w
Edited
Report this post
Companies Have a New AI Problem: Too Many Agents - from The Wall Street Journal and Salesforce MuleSoft can absolutely help: https://lnkd.in/eyPuF7rG
Like
Comment
Share
Copy
LinkedIn
Facebook
To view or add a comment, sign in
Michael Adams - Fractional VP of Sales Vistage TA
2w
Report this post
Ian Smith CA - this is a really important point and one that I think many companies are underestimating right now.
Everyone is racing toward AI adoption and AI agents because of the potential productivity and scaling benefits, but very few organizations are stepping back to ask the governance questions first:
- Who owns the process?
- Who validates the output?
- How are decisions escalated?
- How do you prevent conflicting actions, duplicated work, or unmanaged risk?
Without that structure, AI can very quickly create speed without alignment.
What stood out to me in both the article and your SEG framework is the idea that governance cannot be treated as a separate layer added later. It has to exist in a continuous relationship with strategy and execution or the organization eventually creates noise, redundancy, and operational risk instead of scalable value.
I also think your point about “pace and space” is critical. Smaller and mid-sized companies especially can suffocate themselves by overengineering governance, while others create almost none at all. The balance is what matters.
The companies that win with AI won’t just be the ones that deploy the most tools. They’ll be the ones that create the clearest operating model around how those tools support strategy, execution, accountability, and decision-making.
Ian Smith CA
Creating Successful Acquirers. As an operator scaled 17 businesses, 8 exits. As an Investment Banker bought & sold 30 Cos. Pioneer of The Saleability Test. $1Billion value created. World ranked masters track. Vistage TA
2w
Edited
As owners of SMEs consider the potential transformation of their businesses by adopting AI and specifically AI Agents, read this article. It emphasizes the need for Governance. Something we believe is misunderstood in the scaling process.
Check out our operating model in the comments and how it captures the symbiotic relationship between Strategy, Execution and Governance (SEG).

The core issue facing enterprises is not "AI agent sprawl," but rather unmanaged digital actors that possess autonomy, context, and identity, creating risk when increasing autonomy meets decreasing visibility. To manage this, agents must be treated as enterprise actors with governance built on four pillars: orchestration, lineage, execution controls, and validation.

Michael Adams - Fractional VP of Sales Vistage TA
2w
Report this post
Ian Smith CA - this is a really important point and one that I think many companies are underestimating right now.
Everyone is racing toward AI adoption and AI agents because of the potential productivity and scaling benefits, but very few organizations are stepping back to ask the governance questions first:
- Who owns the process?
- Who validates the output?
- How are decisions escalated?
- How do you prevent conflicting actions, duplicated work, or unmanaged risk?
Without that structure, AI can very quickly create speed without alignment.
What stood out to me in both the article and your SEG framework is the idea that governance cannot be treated as a separate layer added later. It has to exist in a continuous relationship with strategy and execution or the organization eventually creates noise, redundancy, and operational risk instead of scalable value.
I also think your point about “pace and space” is critical. Smaller and mid-sized companies especially can suffocate themselves by overengineering governance, while others create almost none at all. The balance is what matters.
The companies that win with AI won’t just be the ones that deploy the most tools. They’ll be the ones that create the clearest operating model around how those tools support strategy, execution, accountability, and decision-making.
